title: Iran Sports Minister: 'Maybe the Decision is Not to Go' — Participation Hinges on US Safety Guarantees With 51 Days to Kickoff
detail: Iran's Sports and Youth Minister Ahmad Donyamali issued a markedly cautious public statement on April 21, saying 'We have to be ready, but maybe the decision is not to go' — the most explicit acknowledgment yet from Iran's government that non-participation remains a live option. Donyamali stated that Iran will only travel to the United States if verifiable player safety guarantees are provided, and emphasized that the final decision rests with the government and the Supreme National Security Council rather than the football federation. The statement came two weeks after the April 8 ceasefire brokered by Pakistan, which had briefly raised hopes that Iran's security concerns might soften. FIFA President Infantino had declared Iran 'coming for sure' on April 18 at CNBC's Invest in America Forum — directly contradicted by Donyamali's language 72 hours later. The FIFA Congress in Vancouver on April 30 — nine days away — remains the formal decision point, where Iran's Group G schedule (June 15 vs New Zealand at SoFi Stadium, LA; June 21 vs Belgium at SoFi; June 26 vs Egypt at Lumen Field, Seattle) remains officially unchanged. Iran's training base at Kino Sports Complex in Tucson, Arizona was confirmed earlier in April despite the political uncertainty.

media.caption: Iran Sports Minister Ahmad Donyamali says 'maybe the decision is not to go' — Iran's participation in the 2026 World Cup remains unresolved 51 days from kickoff

title: Hisense Celebrates 51-Day Countdown at FIFA HQ Zurich; Named Exclusive VAR Review TV Provider for 2026 Tournament
detail: Official FIFA partner Hisense held a major launch event at the Home of FIFA in Zurich on April 21, marking approximately 51 days to the June 11 kickoff at Estadio Azteca. The event unveiled Hisense's new RGB MiniLED TV range and confirmed the company as the exclusive VAR (Video Assistant Referee) Review Television Provider for the 2026 FIFA World Cup — the first time a consumer electronics brand has been designated the sole display provider for FIFA's controversial VAR system. Hisense's display technology will be installed in FIFA's Video Operation Room, where match officials review on-field decisions. The partnership underscores the scale of commercial investment in the first 48-team World Cup: Hisense is one of FIFA's seven top-tier 'Vision Partners,' a global sponsor category. The event highlighted the accelerating commercial and technical countdown to the tournament.

media.caption: Hisense's FIFA World Cup 2026 countdown event at the Home of FIFA in Zurich — the company is the exclusive VAR Review TV provider for the tournament
